coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots with aggregation-induced emission enhancement (AIEE) for detection of Zr4+/ Hf4+ and PTP1B activity
coredata.dc:creator: Jin, Lei
Yang, Jinmei
Zhang, Wenfeng
Liu, Huan
Mou, Senfeng
Hui, Qi
coredata.dc:description: The frequent use of Zirconium (Zr) and Hafnium (Hf) in modern industries may result in serious environment issues, and thus developing analytical methods to facilitate the control of these two resembled metal elements is urgently needed. However, up to now, rapidly and conveniently detecting Zr4+ and Hf4+ is still full of challenge. In this study, nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N, S-Cdots) with aggregation-induced emission enhancement (AIEE) were prepared and used for turn-on detection of Zr4+ and Hf4+ within 10 min. The photoluminescent intensity of N, S-Cdots showed a good linear correlation with Zr4+ and Hf4+concentrations ranging from 0 to 30 μM with the limit of detection (LOD) of 0.47 and 0.53 μM, respectively. Furthermore, this fluorometric assay was successfully used for quantitative analysis of Zr4+ and Hf4+ in real water samples with satisfactory recoveries in the range of 91.16–108.50% and 90.28–106.49%, respectively. Finally, the developed N, S-Cdots sensing system was used for assay PTP1B activity and screening its inhibitor with Zr4+ as the medium. Our work demonstrated that the as-prepared N, S-Cdots with AIEE can offer a simple and reliable alternative for rapid detection of Zr4+ and Hf4+ in water samples, in addition to being potential useful in phosphatase analysis and drug discovery.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Aggregation-induced emission enhancement (AIEE)
Zr4+
Hf4+
PTP1B

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: PL intensity of N, S-Cdots responded to different metal ions
PTB1B activity assay and drug screening

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang province
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang province, China (Grant No. LQ23H300006 ; Grant No. Y21H150027 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization results of N, S-Cdots. (a) TEM image. (b) Particle size distribution. (c) HRTEM image of single particle. (d) AFM image. (e) FTIR spectrum. (f) XPS full survey.

Characterization of the AIEE phenomenon of N, S-Cdots. (a) PL spectra of N, S-Cdots treated with of Zr4+ from 0 to 0.15 mM. (b) PL intensity of N, S-Cdots treated with Zr4+ from 0 to 0.15 mM (Excitation wavelength, 330 nm; emission wavelength, 420 nm). (c) TEM image of N, S-Cdots. (d) TEM image of N, S-Cdots interacted with 1 mM Zr4+. (e) Zeta potentials of N, S-Cdots interacted with Zr4+ and PO4 3−. (f) TEM image of N, S-Cdots interacted with 0.1 mM Zr4+ and 0.05 mM PO4 3−. The error bars represent the standard deviation of three measurements.

PL intensity of N, S-Cdots measured in different solvents and temperature. (a) In different glycerol-water mixtures at room temperature. (b) In 50% glycerol-water mixture after warming-up from −20 °C to room temperature. The error bars represent the standard deviation of three measurements. The excitation/emission wavelengths of N, S-Cdots and FITC were 330/420 nm and 480/515 nm, respectively.

Selectivity and sensitivity for detection of Zr4+ and Hf4+. (a) The selectivity investigation. All metal ions were 25 μM (Mix 1 contained Zr4+ and other metal ions; Mix 2 contained Hf4+ and other metal ions; Mix 3 contained other metal ions without Zr4+/Hf4+). (b-c) The linear curve for Zr4+ and Hf4+ detection, respectively. The error bars represent the standard deviation of three measurements (excitation wavelength, 330 nm; emission wavelength, 420 nm).

Analytical performance of the developed N, S-Cdots-Zr4+ sensing system for the detection of PTP1B activity and inhibitor screening. (a) PL spectra of the system interacted with different concentrations of PTP1B (0–0.20 μg mL−1). (b) The linear curve for PTP1B concentration detection. (c) PL spectra of the system reacted with 0.12 μg mL−1 PTP1B and different concentrations of oleanolic acid. (d) Relationship between I/I0 and different oleanolic acid concentration. The error bars represent the standard deviation of three measurements (excitation wavelength, 330 nm; emission wavelength, 420 nm).
Schematic illustration of N, S-Cdots fluorescent probe for detection of Zr4+/Hf4+ and PTP1B activity.
Analytical performance of proposed method for quantitative determination of Zr4+ and Hf4+ in actual samples.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots with aggregation-induced emission enhancement (AIEE) for detection of Zr4+/ Hf4+ and PTP1B activity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Wenzhou Medical University, Wenzhou, 325035, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Wenzhou Medical University, Wenzhou 325035, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Biomedical Engineering, School of Ophthalmology and Optometry, Eye Hospital, Wenzhou Medical University, Wenzhou, 325001, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Biomedical Engineering, School of Ophthalmology and Optometry, Eye Hospital, Wenzhou Medical University, Wenzhou 325001, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The frequent use of Zirconium (Zr) and Hafnium (Hf) in modern industries may result in serious environment issues, and thus developing analytical methods to facilitate the control of these two resembled metal elements is urgently needed. However, up to now, rapidly and conveniently detecting Zr4+ and Hf4+ is still full of challenge. In this study, nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N, S-Cdots) with aggregation-induced emission enhancement (AIEE) were prepared and used for turn-on detection of Zr4+ and Hf4+ within 10 min. The photoluminescent intensity of N, S-Cdots showed a good linear correlation with Zr4+ and Hf4+concentrations ranging from 0 to 30 μM with the limit of detection (LOD) of 0.47 and 0.53 μM, respectively. Furthermore, this fluorometric assay was successfully used for quantitative analysis of Zr4+ and Hf4+ in real water samples with satisfactory recoveries in the range of 91.16–108.50% and 90.28–106.49%, respectively. Finally, the developed N, S-Cdots sensing system was used for assay PTP1B activity and screening its inhibitor with Zr4+ as the medium. Our work demonstrated that the as-prepared N, S-Cdots with AIEE can offer a simple and reliable alternative for rapid detection of Zr4+ and Hf4+ in water samples, in addition to being potential useful in phosphatase analysis and drug discovery.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Zirconium (Zr) and Hafnium (Hf) are two resembled metal elements that are widely used in chemical and nuclear industries [1–4]. However, their frequent use can result in high levels of residual Zr4+ and Hf4+, which may result in serious environment issues [5]. Therefore, the development of methods for sensitively and selectively detection of Zr4+ and Hf4+ is urgent needed in modern analytical science. Nowadays, some instrumental methods have been proposed for detection of Zr4+ and Hf4+, such as inductively coupled plasma mass analysis (ICP-MS) [6], laser-induced fluorescence spectrometry [7], glow discharge mass spectrometry (GD-MS) [8], reversed-phase liquid chromatography (RP-LC) [9], etc.. However, these methods required precision instruments, complex or expensive procedures and professional operations. Recently, some organic fluorophores were developed for detection of Zr4+ [10,11]. However, the methodologies developed using such techniques often appear to face complex synthetic procedures and also suffer from some disadvantages, including the toxicity of the probe itself, and high susceptibility to photobleaching. Moreover, to the best of our knowledge, there was no reported probes for detection of Hf4+ yet. Consequently, developing a simple, rapid, cost-effectiveness, convenient and green method for detection of Zr4+ and Hf4+ is still highly desired.

Carbon dots (Cdots) as an emerging member of the fluorescent nanomaterials have attracted extensive interest since their discovery [12–14]. Due to the π-systems as well as the rich carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the edges and surfaces of Cdots, metal ions can easily interact and coordinate with them, which significantly changed the photoluminescent (PL) intensity of Cdots. Based on these merits, various Cdots-based fluorescent probes have been fabricated for detection of metal ions via static quenching [15], inner filter effect (IFE) [16], electronic transfer [17], etc.. However, most of these methods were based on fluorescence-quenching strategy. When compared with “turn-off” probes, fluorescent “turn-on” probes were much more preferred because they could reduce false-positive signals and improve the sensitivity [18]. To address this issue, some Cdots with aggregation-induced emission enhancement (AIEE) have been constructed for turn-on sensing of metal ions [19–21]. The AIEE phenomenon was first reported by Tang group in 2001 [22] and has since received great attention for its unique properties. Unlike the conventional fluorophores which suffered from aggregation-caused quenching effect, AIEE luminogens had weak emission in dissolved state, but showed stronger emission when molecules were aggregated due to the restriction of the intramolecular motion [23]. Nevertheless, the development and application of AIEE-based Cdots is still in their early stage and great efforts are needed to make the best use of their excellent structural and optical properties in meal ions detection.

By taking into account the above considerations, we herein reported nitrogen and sulfur co-doped Cdots (N, S-Cdots) with AIEE for detection of Zr4+and Hf4+. Firstly, N, S-Cdots with AIEE were unexpectedly obtained via a hydrothermal method and their chemical structure were systematically characterized and discussed. Then, PL intensity of N, S-Cdots interacted with different metal ions were evaluated, which provided basis for selectively detection of Zr4+/Hf4+. The analytical performances were furtherly assessed in terms of linear ranges, limit of detection (LOD), accuracy and precision. Finally, the N, S-Cdots sensing system with Zr4+ as medium was proposed for assay phosphatase activity and screening its inhibitor with protein tyrosine phosphatase 1B (PTP1B) as a research case. PTP1B is a major negative regulator of the insulin signal pathways, and its inhibitors were anticipated to be used for treating type 2 diabetes [24] (Scheme 1 ). The combined results demonstrated that the as-prepared N, S-Cdots was not only a promising alternative for rapidly detecting Zr4+/Hf4+ in water samples, but also showed great potential for phosphatase analysis and the phosphatase-targeted drug discovery.

The details of materials and experiment sections were seen in Supporting
Information .

N, S-Cdots were prepared by a one-pot hydrothermal method with citric acid and thiourea as the precursor. The surface morphology and particle size distribution of as-prepared N, S-Cdots were characterized by TEM and AFM images. TEM images showed the particles were uniform and well-dispersed with a relatively narrow size distribution ranging from 2 to 6 nm with an average diameter of 4.12 nm (Fig. 1 a and Fig. 1b). The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image indicated the high crystallinity of the N, S-Cdots and displayed a lattice spacing distance of 2.0 Å (Fig. 1c), which is consistent with the (100) diffraction planes of sp2 graphitic carbon, implying that N, S-Cdots maintained similar crystallinity to graphene [25]. AFM image (Fig. 1d) showed their average height was 2.57 nm, suggested that most of the crystalized N, S-Cdots had a five-layers stacked structure [26]. In XRD (Fig. S1), the broad diffraction peak at ∼21° which corresponded to (100) plane of carbon, endorsed the formation of sp2 amorphous graphitic carbon [25].

FT-IR spectrum was recorded to determine the surface functional groups of N, S-Cdots (Fig. 1e). A broad absorption band at 3200∼3419 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibrations of O–H and N–H groups [27]. The peaks at about 1715 cm−1 indicated the existence of COOH. The band at 1587 cm−1 can be assigned to the CC stretching mode of the aromatic rings. While the band at 1402 cm−1 were from the bending vibrations of C–N and symmetric stretching vibrations of COO−. The peaks at ∼1187 and 1048 cm−1 were attributed to the COC bending vibration [28]. Additionally, XPS measurements were utilized to confirm the surface structure. As illustrated in Fig. 1f, the full range XPS showed two significant peaks at 284 and 532 eV, which can be assigned as C1s and O1s, respectively. Two small peaks of N1s (399 eV) and S2p (164 eV) were also noticed in the full survey scan of N, S-Cdots, which confirmed the successful co-doping of N and S atoms with the amounts calculated as 0.51 and 0.49 wt% according to CasaXPS software. The corresponding binding energies were further confirmed by their high-resolution XPS spectra (Fig. S2). The high-resolution XPS of the C1s band was deconvoluted into two peaks at 283.0 and 286.6 eV which were assigned to CC/C–C and C–O bonds, respectively [29]. The high-resolution scan of the N1s region showed that nitrogen was present in two different chemical environments, corresponding to pyrrolic N (C–N–C) at 398.8 eV and graphitic N or N–H bands at 401.6 eV [30]. The high-resolution spectrum S2p showed two peaks centered about at 161.9 eV and 166.7 eV, which belongs to the 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 position of the-C-S-C- covalent bond of thiophene-like S due to the spin-orbit couplings [31].

The optical properties of N, S-Cdots were also investigated. N, S-Cdots showed a broad UV–vis absorption spectrum, where the strong absorption peak was observed at 337 nm, ascribing to the n-π* transition of CO and π-π* transition of CC (Fig. S3a) [32]. The fluorescence spectra of N, S-Cdots with variations of excitation wavelength (300–380 nm) were recorded (Fig. S3b). When the excitation wavelength was 330 nm, a strong PL emission peak located at 435 nm was observed with a quantum yield (QY) of 1.80% against quinine sulphate as the reference. Like most reported carbon dots, N, S-Cdots also exhibited excitation-dependent PL behavior. In addition, the effects of pH value on the PL stability of N, S-Cdots were evaluated (Fig. S4), which showed that the strongest PL intensity appeared when pH was about 7.5. This pH-dependent emission feature of N, S-Cdots was mainly relative with the protonation and deprotonation of the functional groups on N, S-Cdots surface. In this study, the Tris-HCl buffer solution (pH = 7.4) was used for the whole experiment.

Metal ions were often used as external factors to induce the aggregation of Cdots through coordination and electrostatic interaction. In this study, different concentrations of Zr4+ were firstly added to the N, S-Cdots solution to investigate the PL behavior. As shown in Fig. 2 a and 2b, PL intensity of N, S-Cdots significantly improved with the increasing Zr4+ concentration and became stable when Zr4+ was up to 0.1 mM. This phenomenon was quite different from these reported literatures, where an introduced of the Zr4+ could significantly reduce the PL intensity of Cdots [33,34].

To seek the possible reasons, the morphology and the surface charge of N, S-Cdots with/without the presence of Zr4+ were subsequently characterized by the TEM and Zeta potential experiments. Before Zr4+ was added, N, S-Cdots were monodisperse (Fig. 2c), while an added of 0.1 mM Zr4+ caused significant aggregation (Fig. 2d). The zeta potentials showed that N, S-Cdots had a considerable negative charge of −14.5 mV, while the negative charge became great positive of 30.4 mV after reacting with 0.1 mM Zr4+, which indicated that Zr4+ was binding well with N, S-Cdots (Fig. 2e). Given these facts, it is speculated that the strong emission of N, S-Cdots in the presence of Zr4+ should be ascribed to the AIEE effect. Due to the presence of both sp2 carbon and sp3 hybridized carbon in the N, S-Cdots, the luminescence center possessing unplanar conformations and may undergo low-frequency twisting and torsional motions in disperse condition, which serve as a relaxation channel for the excited state to decay non-radiatively. When Zr4+ was added, it can bind to the surface and induce the aggregation of N, S-Cdots. In the aggregated state, these motions were suppressed due to the spatial constraint, which blocked the radiationless relaxation channel and actuated the energy release through radiative pathway, thus enhancing PL intensity.

As N, S-Cdots and PO4 3− had competitive interaction for Zr4+, PL intensity of N, S-Cdots would change if PO4 3− was added to N, S-Cdots-Zr4+ mixed solution. To test this hypothesis, different concentrations of PO4 3− were added to N, S-Cdots-Zr4+ solution. The results showed that when PO4 3− increased from 0 to 20 μM, PL intensity had a moderate growth; but when the concentration increased from 20 μM to 80 μM, PL intensity had a sharply decreases (Fig. S5). The first increasing part can be explained that the previous original water molecules coordinated with Zr4+ was replaced by PO4 3− and increased the rotation resistance, and thus enhancing the PL intensity. However, when PO4 3− was excessive, they competitively interacted with Zr4+, removed Zr4+ from the surface of the N, S-Cdots, which leaded N, S-Cdots in disaggregate state and decreased the PL intensity. The aggregation and the surface charge of N, S-Cdots-Zr4+ mixed solution in the presence PO4 3− were also identified by the TEM and Zeta potential experiments. An obvious disaggregate state could be found after 50 μM PO4 3− was added (Fig. 2f), while the zeta potentials changed from 30.4 mV to 1.88 mV. All these above results confirmed that our hypothesis that N, S-Cdots had AIEE effect after Zr4+ were introduced was correct.

To furtherly confirm the AIEE, PL intensity of N, S-Cdots under different viscosity solvents and temperature were also evaluated, where the two factors were often used to manipulated the restriction of intramolecular motions or rotations for AIEE-based molecules. PL intensity of N, S-Cdots was firstly investigated by diluted them in glycerol/water (v/v) mixed solution. The results showed that PL intensity of N, S-Cdots increased more than 8-fold as the glycerol fraction increased from 0 to 90 vol %; while no such enhancement was observed when a conventional organic dye, i.e. fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC), was used (Fig. 3 a). Then, temperature effect on PL intensity was checked. N, S-Cdots dissolved in glycerol/H2O (v/v = 1/1) mixed solution was firstly kept in −20 °C for 30 min and subsequently taken out in microplate reader and PL intensity was recorded during the warming-up process with the interval of 0.5 min. The results showed that PL intensity decreased sharply as the temperature increased, while for FITC, PL intensity had little change (Fig. 3b). The above results demonstrated that increasing the solvent viscosity and decreasing the environmental temperature could significantly improve the PL intensity of N, S-Cdots, which could be attributed to the restricted intramolecular motions or rotations.

Finally, whether carbon dots prepared by other reaction conditions having the similar AIEE were also evaluated (Fig. S6). The results showed that only when the reactants were citric acid and thiourea, and the solvent was water, the obtained carbon dots can have AIEE, in which PL intensity of carbon dots significantly enhanced when 0.1 mM Zr4+ were introduced. The effects of different reactants feeding ratio experiments indicated that high citric acid feeding ratio resulted in high PL intensity enhancement for N, S-Cdots. The possible reason was that the increasing proportion of citric acid increased the surface carboxyl group of N, S-Cdots, which improved coordination ability with Zr4+ and induced the restriction of intramolecular motions or rotations. Besides, the increasing citric acid enhanced the sp3 hybridized carbon ratio in N, S-Cdots, which improved the unplanar conformations and low-frequency twisting and torsional motions. It was noticeable that if only citric acid or thiourea was used, the obtained products showed negligible fluorescence response toward Zr4+. Furthermore, the effect of different reaction time on AIEE was also studied, which demonstrated that a reaction time of 4 h was enough to obtain N, S-Cdots with strong PL enhancement triggered by Zr4+ (Fig. S7). The above experimental results demonstrated the synthesized N, S-Cdots had uniqueness and repeatability AIEE, which was quite important for their applications in biosensing. Interestingly, among all the carbon dots prepared from citric acid and thiourea by varieties of methods, only this work exhibited obvious AIEE, which indicated the significance of the synthesized conditions to the nanoparticle structure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PL intensity of N, S-Cdots responded to different metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: Besides Zr4+, PL intensity of the N, S-Cdots interacted with other metal ions were also investigated. Therefore, 25 μM of different metal ions including Hf4+, Ti4+, Ce4+, Sn4+, Ti3+, La3+, Gd3+, In3+, Al3+, Ga3+, Fe3+, Eu3+, Er3+, Cr3+, Ce3+, Tb3+, Sn2+, Fe2+, Ni2+, Hg2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Cd2+, Ca2+, Na+ and K+ were individually added to N, S-Cdots solution. The PL intensity results indicated that among the investigated metal ions, only Zr4+ and Hf4+ induced a significant increasement (Fig. 4 a). The high selectivity of N, S-Cdots for Zr4+/Hf4+ could be due to the fact that the Zr4+/Hf4+ had a higher binding ability via coordination or electro-static interaction with N, S-Cdots and thus producing the AIEE. Interestingly, Ti4+, Ce4+ and Sn4+ with same high charge was found to reduce PL intensity, which could be explained that in these cases, the electrode potential of metal ion (Ti4+, −4.41 eV; Ce4+, −6.12 eV; Sn4+, −4.55 eV) was less than the energy level of lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (ELUMO) of N, S-Cdots (−4.01 eV, the calculation process was seen in SI ), thus the photoexcited electrons in the N, S-Cdots transfer from lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) to the orbitals of the metal ions with fitting energy, consequently reducing of the PL intensity of N, S-Cdots. It was notable that due to the Zr4+ and Hf4+ having similar chemical properties, they almost had same enhanced ability for N, S-Cdots. Therefore, it is quite difficult to discriminate Zr4+ and Hf4+ if they co-existed in mixture samples, which need extra work to tackle this problem.

Besides, in order to further study the selectivity of N, S-Cdots, the competition experiments were also conducted in the presence of Zr4+/Hf4+ and other ions, which showed that the PL intensity of N, S-Cdots did not significantly change in the presence of other ions. These combined results clearly demonstrated that the detection method based on N, S-Cdots was highly selective for Zr4+/Hf4+.

The above results indicated the potential of N, S-Cdots as an ideal platform for sensing of Zr4+and Hf4+. Under the optimal conditions, good linear relationship was obtained with Zr4+/Hf4+ concentrations in the range from 0 to 30 μM with the correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9976 and 0.9922 for Zr4+and Hf4+, respectively (Fig. 4b and c). The whole detection process could be completed within 10 min with the LOD of 0.47 μM and 0.53 μM for Zr4+ and Hf4+, which were calculated based on 3σ rule and had comparable sensitivity when compared to the reported fluorescent assays (Table S1).

To furtherly evaluate the practical value of this method, different concentrations of Zr4+/Hf4+ were added and detected in actual water samples. The recovery and precision results were shown in Table 1 . A recovery of 90.28–108.50% and precision of 2.24–5.79% were satisfactory for a method to rapidly determine Zr4+ and Hf4+ in practical samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PTB1B activity assay and drug screening
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the above facts, a method for the assay of PTP1B activity and inhibitor screening were proposed by using Zr4+ as the medium. PTP1B can dephosphorylate the substrate (O-phospho-l-tyrosine, pTYR) and produce PO4 3−, which had a competitive binding with Zr4+, thus removed Zr4+ from the surface and reduced the PL intensity. The activity of PTP1B can be easily and proportionally reflected by the change in fluorescence signals.

PL spectra of the system incubated with different concentrations of PTP1B were collected in Fig. 5 a. The results showed that PL intensity decreased gradually as the PTP1B increased from 0 to 0.20 μg mL−1. The detection linear for PTP1B was from 0.02 to 0.16 μg mL−1 with LOD of 0.02 μg mL−1 and R2 of 0.9941 (Fig. 5b). These results demonstrated that this method was satisfactory for the assay of PTP1B activity.

As PTP1B inhibitors may become potential drugs for the treatment of many diseases, the proposed PTP1B assay is further tested and verified for its potential applications in inhibitor screening. Oleanolic acid, which has been recognized as a potent inhibitor of PTP1B, is chosen as a proof-of-concept inhibitor. As was shown in Fig. 5c, PL intensity increased gradually with the concentration of oleanolic acid increasing from 0 to 40 μM. The relationship between I/I0 and oleanolic acid concentrations was plotted in Fig. 5d. The IC50 value is determined to be 7.473 μM, which is well consistent with the reported literature [35]. The results of the inhibition study suggested that the proposed N, S-Cdots-Zr4+ system was suitable for screening potential inhibitors of PTP1B.

Briefly, in this work, nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots with AIEE effect were synthesized and utilized to construct fluorescent nanoprobes for highly sensitive detection of Zr4+/Hf4+ and PTPTB activity. The fluorescence “turn-on” sensing system enabled quick detection of Zr4+ and Hf4+ with high sensitivity and selectivity, and it was successful to detect the two reassemble metal ions in real water samples with satisfactory accuracy and precision results. Compared with the previous fluorometric works, the proposed method does not require time-consuming and toxic organic synthetic procedures, which is quite simple, convenient, economic and green for practical application. Furthermore, it was successful to detect PTP1B activity and screening its inhibitor with Zr4+ as the medium, which also shows its promising application prospects in pharmaceutical analysis.

Lei Jin: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data collection and analysis, Writing-Original draft. Jinmei Yang: Reviewing and Editing, Visualization. Wenfeng Zhang: Validation and Data curation. Huan Liu: Validation and Visualization. Senfeng Mou: Validation. Qi Hui: Formal analysis Investigation, Reviewing and Editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang province, China (Grant No. LQ23H300006; Grant No. Y21H150027).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2023.124527.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Corrosion of zirconium alloys used for nuclear fuel cladding

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.T. Motta, A. Couet, R.J. Comstock, Corrosion of zirconium alloys used for nuclear fuel cladding, Annu. Rev. Mater. Res. 45 (2015) 311-343.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High-purity titanium, zirconium, and hafnium in nuclear power

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.L. Kotsar, S.A. Lavrikov, V.I. Nikonov, A.V. Aleksandrov, S.G. Akhtonov, A.V. Aleksandrov, High-purity titanium, zirconium, and hafnium in nuclear power, Atomic Energy, 111 (2011) 92-98.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The chemistry and applications of hafnium and cerium (IV) metal-organic frameworks

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Hu, Y. Wang, D. Zhao, The chemistry and applications of hafnium and cerium (IV) metal-organic frameworks, Chem. Soc. Rev. 50 (2021) 4629-4683.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: On the melting of zirconium alloys from scraps using electron beam and induction furnaces–recycling process viability

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L.A.T. Pereira, L.G. Martinez, C.S. Mucsi, L.A.M. Reis, J.L. Rossi, On the melting of zirconium alloys from scraps using electron beam and induction furnaces-recycling process viability, J. Mater. Res. Technol, 9 (2020) 4867-4875.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Efficient fluorescence turn-on probe for zirconium via a target-triggered DNA molecular beacon strategy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H.M. Meng, T. Fu, X.B. Zhang, N.N. Wang, W.H. Tan, G.L. Shen, R.Q. Yu, Efficient fluorescence turn-on probe for zirconium via a target-triggered DNA molecular beacon strategy, Anal. Chem. 84 (2012) 2124-2128.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Application of the Zr/Hf ratio in the determination of hafnium in geochemical samples by high-resolution inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.X. Liu, Q.X. Li, N. Ma, X.L. Sun, J.F. Bai, Q. Zhang, Application of the Zr/Hf ratio in the determination of hafnium in geochemical samples by high-resolution inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry, Anal. Chem. 86 (2014) 11570-11577.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Development of laser ablation absorption spectroscopy for nuclear fuel materials: plume expansion behavior for refractory metals observed by laser-induced fluorescence imaging spectroscopy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Miyabe, M. Oba, K. Akaoka, M. Kato, S. Hasegawa, I. Wakaida, Development of laser ablation absorption spectroscopy for nuclear fuel materials: plume expansion behavior for refractory metals observed by laser-induced fluorescence imaging spectroscopy, Applied Physics A, 126 (2020) 1-10.
